trans-unit.source: In the context of the Anticipatory Action methodology the conduction of a robust risk assessment is a crucial step towards the development of an Early Action Protocol. A risk analysis serves to understand what kinds of disaster impacts can be expected from a particular type of hazard and to identify who and what is exposed and vulnerable to this hazard and why. By overlaying the information on exposure, vulnerability and lack of coping capacity, it will become clear which areas are predicted to be most severely impacted. These areas can then be targeted as priority areas for early action to ensure the most at-risk communities receive assistance before the event happens. The collection and processing of this information varies throughout different contexts but the calculation scheme to combine the information to a risk score remains consistent. At HeiGIT we have developed a risk assessment workflow that is applicable across different countries and disaster contexts. To make the process more user-friendly, we have chosen to convert it into a QGIS Plugin. This makes it more accessible and easier to use, especially for individuals with limited technical knowledge. The plugin automatically calculates the risk based on the exposure, vulnerability and coping capacity indicators entered by the user.
trans-unit.target: In the context of the Anticipatory Action methodology the conduction of a robust risk assessment is a crucial step towards the development of an Early Action Protocol. A risk analysis serves to understand what kinds of disaster impacts can be expected from a particular type of hazard and to identify who and what is exposed and vulnerable to this hazard and why. By overlaying the information on exposure, vulnerability and lack of coping capacity, it will become clear which areas are predicted to be most severely impacted. These areas can then be targeted as priority areas for early action to ensure the most at-risk communities receive assistance before the event happens. The collection and processing of this information varies throughout different contexts but the calculation scheme to combine the information to a risk score remains consistent. At HeiGIT we have developed a risk assessment workflow that is applicable across different countries and disaster contexts. To make the process more user-friendly, we have chosen to convert it into a QGIS Plugin. This makes it more accessible and easier to use, especially for individuals with limited technical knowledge. The plugin automatically calculates the risk based on the exposure, vulnerability and coping capacity indicators entered by the user.

trans-unit.source: The use of the plugin requires Python pandas to be installed on your system. You can check in QGIS directly if you have it installed or not:
trans-unit.target: The use of the plugin requires Python pandas to be installed on your system. You can check in QGIS directly if you have it installed or not:

trans-unit.source: In the menu bar click on Plugins
trans-unit.target: In the menu bar click on Plugins

trans-unit.source: In the Python Console type the following command and press ENTER:
trans-unit.target: In the Python Console type the following command and press ENTER:

trans-unit.source: If the library is installed, this command will execute without error, and you won’t see any error messages. If pandas is not installed, you will see error messages indicating that the respective module cannot be found. In that case, you will need to install the missing library. You can install pandas using pip: Install pandas on Windows/ Mac:\
trans-unit.target: If the library is installed, this command will execute without error, and you won’t see any error messages. If pandas is not installed, you will see error messages indicating that the respective module cannot be found. In that case, you will need to install the missing library. You can install pandas using pip: Install pandas on Windows/ Mac:\

trans-unit.source: Install pandas by running the following command and press ENTER:
trans-unit.target: Install pandas by running the following command and press ENTER:

trans-unit.source: The Plugin is available for QGIS Version 3.28 onwards.
trans-unit.target: The Plugin is available for QGIS Version 3.28 onwards.

trans-unit.source: To install plugins, you need to open the Plugin Manager:
trans-unit.target: To install plugins, you need to open the Plugin Manager:

trans-unit.source: Click on the menu item Plugins > Manage and install Plugins.
trans-unit.target: Click on the menu item Plugins > Manage and install Plugins.

trans-unit.source: Click on Settings and check the box “Show also experimental plugins”
trans-unit.target: Click on Settings and check the box “Show also experimental plugins”

trans-unit.source: In the dialog that opens, find the Risk Assessment plugin.
trans-unit.target: In the dialog that opens, find the Risk Assessment plugin.

trans-unit.source: Find information about the plugin by selecting it in the list.
trans-unit.target: Find information about the plugin by selecting it in the list.

trans-unit.source: Install it by clicking the Install Plugin button below the plugin information panel.
trans-unit.target: Install it by clicking the Install Plugin button below the plugin information panel.

trans-unit.source: Close the dialog. The Risk Assessment plugin should now be available.
trans-unit.target: Close the dialog. The Risk Assessment plugin should now be available.

trans-unit.source: For further information about installation and usage of plugins visit the [QGIS Training Platform](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_plugins_wiki.html).
trans-unit.target: For further information about installation and usage of plugins visit the [QGIS Training Platform](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_plugins_wiki.html).

trans-unit.source: How to open the plugin interface:
trans-unit.target: How to open the plugin interface:

trans-unit.source: To use the Risk Assessment plugin open the `Processing Toolbox`.
trans-unit.target: To use the Risk Assessment plugin open the `Processing Toolbox`.

trans-unit.source: The Toolbox shows a list of all available algorithms.
trans-unit.target: The Toolbox shows a list of all available algorithms.

trans-unit.source: At the bottom it should list `HeiGIT Risk Assessment`. you can also search for “Risk Assessment” using the Search function of the Processing Toolbox
trans-unit.target: At the bottom it should list `HeiGIT Risk Assessment`. you can also search for “Risk Assessment” using the Search function of the Processing Toolbox

trans-unit.source: To execute the plugin, just double-click on its name.
trans-unit.target: To execute the plugin, just double-click on its name.

trans-unit.source: A dialog called Calculate Risk Assessment should appear.
trans-unit.target: A dialog called Calculate Risk Assessment should appear.

trans-unit.source: The plugin needs 5 inputs, from which 4 are csv text files and 1 is a vector file. The entry of 4 of these 5 files is mandatory. The coping capacity file is optional, but is strongly recommended for a more comprehensive and realistic risk assessment. The majority of the files can be produced and adjusted in Excel. This allows for flexibility to adjust input to local contexts. The requested input information can be browsed for via the button on the right. These files need to be present on the user's computer. It is important to follow the input file specifications exactly (see chapter 4.1.). The plugin will provide the user with two outputs. The Risk Assessment results on the administrative boundaries as a vector file containing geometries ready to be displayed in QGIS and the Risk Assessment results in a table format. The desired data format of the vector and text outputs can be chosen during the saving process.
trans-unit.target: The plugin needs 5 inputs, from which 4 are csv text files and 1 is a vector file. The entry of 4 of these 5 files is mandatory. The coping capacity file is optional, but is strongly recommended for a more comprehensive and realistic risk assessment. The majority of the files can be produced and adjusted in Excel. This allows for flexibility to adjust input to local contexts. The requested input information can be browsed for via the button on the right. These files need to be present on the user's computer. It is important to follow the input file specifications exactly (see chapter 4.1.). The plugin will provide the user with two outputs. The Risk Assessment results on the administrative boundaries as a vector file containing geometries ready to be displayed in QGIS and the Risk Assessment results in a table format. The desired data format of the vector and text outputs can be chosen during the saving process.

trans-unit.source: The required input files must follow a given structure. Here you find the required input files to conduct the risk assessment and specifications they must follow:
trans-unit.target: The required input files must follow a given structure. Here you find the required input files to conduct the risk assessment and specifications they must follow:

trans-unit.source: A geospatial vector format (geojson, geopackage, shapefile,…) containing the administrative boundaries and P-Codes of the respective countries. These can be found on the websites of national governments or on Humanitarian Data Exchange, for example. This dataset has two obligatory columns: “ADM_PCODE” containing the P_Codes and “ADM_EN” containing the names of the administrative boundaries. The administrative boundary data does not require a specific coordinate reference system (CRS), but the output and result will have the same CRS as the input.
trans-unit.target: A geospatial vector format (geojson, geopackage, shapefile,…) containing the administrative boundaries and P-Codes of the respective countries. These can be found on the websites of national governments or on Humanitarian Data Exchange, for example. This dataset has two obligatory columns: “ADM_PCODE” containing the P_Codes and “ADM_EN” containing the names of the administrative boundaries. The administrative boundary data does not require a specific coordinate reference system (CRS), but the output and result will have the same CRS as the input.

trans-unit.source: For the Risk Assessment indicators the following 3 “csv”-files are mandatory:
trans-unit.target: For the Risk Assessment indicators the following 3 “csv”-files are mandatory:

trans-unit.source: a) __Exposure indicators:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the exposure-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM..." (for example “ADM_NAMES”)./
trans-unit.target: a) __Exposure indicators:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the exposure-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM..." (for example “ADM_NAMES”)./

trans-unit.source: b.) __Vulnerability indicators:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the vulnerability-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM..."./
trans-unit.target: b.) __Vulnerability indicators:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the vulnerability-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM..."./

trans-unit.source: c) __Coping Capacity indicators [optional]:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the coping-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM...".
trans-unit.target: c) __Coping Capacity indicators [optional]:__ A "csv"-file containing a mandatory column "ADM_PCODE" with the P_codes of the administrative boundaries and all columns that are included in the calculation of the coping-indicator. All columns that are not included in the calculation must start with the expression "ADM...".

trans-unit.source: Further information can be found in the [How to generate Risk Indicators](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_generate_indicators_for_plugin.html) chapter.
trans-unit.target: Further information can be found in the [How to generate Risk Indicators](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_generate_indicators_for_plugin.html) chapter.

trans-unit.source: A "csv"-file containing a column "variable_name" with all the column names of the previous "csv"-files, which are to be weighted differently in the calculation. The file must also contain the columns “category”, “weight” and “direction”. The category column indicates the category of the indicator: “exp” for exposure, “cop” for coping capacity and “vul” for vulnerability. The weight determines the importance of each indicator. For each category (exp, cop and vul), the sum of the weights must add up to 1. If this is not the case, the plugin will automatically normalize the weights in order to make the sum equal to 1 within each category. A message will be printed with the value of the transformed weights. For more information about weights and directions see chapter weight and directions.
trans-unit.target: A "csv"-file containing a column "variable_name" with all the column names of the previous "csv"-files, which are to be weighted differently in the calculation. The file must also contain the columns “category”, “weight” and “direction”. The category column indicates the category of the indicator: “exp” for exposure, “cop” for coping capacity and “vul” for vulnerability. The weight determines the importance of each indicator. For each category (exp, cop and vul), the sum of the weights must add up to 1. If this is not the case, the plugin will automatically normalize the weights in order to make the sum equal to 1 within each category. A message will be printed with the value of the transformed weights. For more information about weights and directions see chapter weight and directions.

trans-unit.source: When you install the Risk Assessment plugin, another plugin is automatically installed alongside it: the WeightsGenerator plugin. This tool allows you to manually assign weights and directions to indicators, without the need to load a pre-prepared weights CSV file.
trans-unit.target: When you install the Risk Assessment plugin, another plugin is automatically installed alongside it: the WeightsGenerator plugin. This tool allows you to manually assign weights and directions to indicators, without the need to load a pre-prepared weights CSV file.

trans-unit.source: To verify that the plugin was installed correctly, look for the following icon on the QGIS toolbar:
trans-unit.target: To verify that the plugin was installed correctly, look for the following icon on the QGIS toolbar:

trans-unit.source: Clicking the icon opens a window where you can browse exposure, coping, and vulnerability indicators using the button on the right. Once selected, the indicators will appear in the table below.
trans-unit.target: Clicking the icon opens a window where you can browse exposure, coping, and vulnerability indicators using the button on the right. Once selected, the indicators will appear in the table below.

trans-unit.source: You can manually enter the weight and direction values in the corresponding rows. If you click the "Default weights" button in the bottom-right corner, each indicator will be assigned a weight value of 1. This means that no weighting is applied—all indicators are treated as equally important in the risk calculation.
trans-unit.target: You can manually enter the weight and direction values in the corresponding rows. If you click the "Default weights" button in the bottom-right corner, each indicator will be assigned a weight value of 1. This means that no weighting is applied—all indicators are treated as equally important in the risk calculation.

trans-unit.source: By clicking "OK", you save the weights file locally as a CSV file. At the same time, it will appear as a layer in the QGIS Layers panel.
trans-unit.target: By clicking "OK", you save the weights file locally as a CSV file. At the same time, it will appear as a layer in the QGIS Layers panel.

trans-unit.source: In the bottom-left corner of the window, there is also a "Load Weights File" button. This allows you to load an existing weights CSV file, which you can edit—for example, by changing individual values or adding new indicators.
trans-unit.target: In the bottom-left corner of the window, there is also a "Load Weights File" button. This allows you to load an existing weights CSV file, which you can edit—for example, by changing individual values or adding new indicators.

trans-unit.source: The plugin provides the user with the following output: Geospatial data format to be chosen by user (geojson, shapefile, geopackage) with the administrative boundaries for the respective country including the following values for each polygon:
trans-unit.target: The plugin provides the user with the following output: Geospatial data format to be chosen by user (geojson, shapefile, geopackage) with the administrative boundaries for the respective country including the following values for each polygon:

trans-unit.source: The output data will contain No Data Values. By default in QGIS these Values are shown as “-999”. These no data values mean that there is no exposure and consequently no risk for the concerned districts.
trans-unit.target: The output data will contain No Data Values. By default in QGIS these Values are shown as “-999”. These no data values mean that there is no exposure and consequently no risk for the concerned districts.

trans-unit.source: The methodology for the Risk Calculation is based on [Weltrisikoindex](https://weltrisikobericht.de/) and is also inspired by the [INFORM Risk framework](drmkc.jrc.ec.europa.eu/inform-index/INFORM-Risk/Methodology). The basic model of the WorldRiskIndex with its modular structure was developed jointly with the United Nations University Institute for Environment and Human Security (UNU-EHS). Since 2018, the Institute for International Law of Peace and Humanitarian Law (IFHV) at the Ruhr University Bochum has taken over the calculation and continuously developed the model conceptually and methodologically. In the context of this analysis, risk is defined as the interaction of the two dimensions of exposure and susceptibility, which arises only where the two spheres meet. In this respect, risk is only present where there are hazards from extreme natural events and where populations without sufficient resilience, coping or adaptation capacities live in these hazard areas.
trans-unit.target: The methodology for the Risk Calculation is based on [Weltrisikoindex](https://weltrisikobericht.de/) and is also inspired by the [INFORM Risk framework](drmkc.jrc.ec.europa.eu/inform-index/INFORM-Risk/Methodology). The basic model of the WorldRiskIndex with its modular structure was developed jointly with the United Nations University Institute for Environment and Human Security (UNU-EHS). Since 2018, the Institute for International Law of Peace and Humanitarian Law (IFHV) at the Ruhr University Bochum has taken over the calculation and continuously developed the model conceptually and methodologically. In the context of this analysis, risk is defined as the interaction of the two dimensions of exposure and susceptibility, which arises only where the two spheres meet. In this respect, risk is only present where there are hazards from extreme natural events and where populations without sufficient resilience, coping or adaptation capacities live in these hazard areas.

trans-unit.source: Depending on the context and content of the data, the value range of the indicators is wide. In order to make them comparable, all indicators are normalized to a value range between 0 to 1. Each value is scaled so that the minimum value in the series becomes 0, and the maximum value becomes 1. Values in between are linearly scaled based on their position within the range. A common Min-Max Normalization is used:
trans-unit.target: Depending on the context and content of the data, the value range of the indicators is wide. In order to make them comparable, all indicators are normalized to a value range between 0 to 1. Each value is scaled so that the minimum value in the series becomes 0, and the maximum value becomes 1. Values in between are linearly scaled based on their position within the range. A common Min-Max Normalization is used:

trans-unit.source: $ Normalized\ Value\ = \frac{value\ -\ min value}{max\ value \ - \ min } $
trans-unit.target: $ Normalized\ Value\ = \frac{value\ -\ min value}{max\ value \ - \ min } $

trans-unit.source: If indicators present missing values, no index values can be calculated for the respective admin boundary, so they would have to be removed from the ranking. To avoid this, the approach makes a pessimistic assumption for missing values in the categories Vulnerability and Coping Capacity: Missing values are assigned the worst circumstances for the respective category (for example highly vulnerable or very low coping capacity). For the category exposure, it is not possible to assume pessimistic circumstances since the risk highly depends on an existing exposure. Basically without exposure there is no risk (e.g. non existent river = non existent riverine flood risk). If we do not have values for exposure we will therefore have to assume that there is no information or no exposure in order not to falsify the result and assume a high exposure in an area where there is none. Since the input data does not consider time series, it is not feasible to make realistic estimates. Remembering the logic explained earlier this results in the following substitution of missing values (each representing the worst possible value):
trans-unit.target: If indicators present missing values, no index values can be calculated for the respective admin boundary, so they would have to be removed from the ranking. To avoid this, the approach makes a pessimistic assumption for missing values in the categories Vulnerability and Coping Capacity: Missing values are assigned the worst circumstances for the respective category (for example highly vulnerable or very low coping capacity). For the category exposure, it is not possible to assume pessimistic circumstances since the risk highly depends on an existing exposure. Basically without exposure there is no risk (e.g. non existent river = non existent riverine flood risk). If we do not have values for exposure we will therefore have to assume that there is no information or no exposure in order not to falsify the result and assume a high exposure in an area where there is none. Since the input data does not consider time series, it is not feasible to make realistic estimates. Remembering the logic explained earlier this results in the following substitution of missing values (each representing the worst possible value):

trans-unit.source: In order to calculate the overall score for each dimension, indicator weights and directions are introduced. The indicators for the three dimensions Exposure, Vulnerability and Coping Capacity receive different weighting and directions which depend on local and expert knowledge and are adjustable.
trans-unit.target: In order to calculate the overall score for each dimension, indicator weights and directions are introduced. The indicators for the three dimensions Exposure, Vulnerability and Coping Capacity receive different weighting and directions which depend on local and expert knowledge and are adjustable.

trans-unit.source: The weights represent the importance of the respective indicator and needs to be defined by the user. The default value is 1 (no weighting). The weights can be chosen and adjusted by the user in the Weights-file. Nevertheless, we recommend the following weights structure ranging between less important and very important (0.25, 0.5, 0,75, 1):
trans-unit.target: The weights represent the importance of the respective indicator and needs to be defined by the user. The default value is 1 (no weighting). The weights can be chosen and adjusted by the user in the Weights-file. Nevertheless, we recommend the following weights structure ranging between less important and very important (0.25, 0.5, 0,75, 1):

trans-unit.source: For each category, the sum of the weights must add up to 1. If not, the plugin will automatically normalize the weights in order to make the sum equal to 1 among weights belonging to the same category (exp cop and vul respectively).
trans-unit.target: For each category, the sum of the weights must add up to 1. If not, the plugin will automatically normalize the weights in order to make the sum equal to 1 among weights belonging to the same category (exp cop and vul respectively).

trans-unit.source: The direction indicates if an indicator follows the predefined logic: “the higher the value, the worse the circumstances” meaning that higher values would result in a higher risk. The logic is adapted for all three dimensions, since it is generally logical to think about high values = high risk. If a respective indicator follows the logic the direction would be 1(default), if it does not, the direction would be = -1.
trans-unit.target: The direction indicates if an indicator follows the predefined logic: “the higher the value, the worse the circumstances” meaning that higher values would result in a higher risk. The logic is adapted for all three dimensions, since it is generally logical to think about high values = high risk. If a respective indicator follows the logic the direction would be 1(default), if it does not, the direction would be = -1.

trans-unit.source: the __higher__ the value the worse the circumstances
trans-unit.target: the __higher__ the value the worse the circumstances

trans-unit.source: the __lower__ the value the worse the circumstances or the higher the values the better the circumstances
trans-unit.target: the __lower__ the value the worse the circumstances or the higher the values the better the circumstances

trans-unit.source: __Example 1:__ If the indicator “Rural Accessibility Index” (proportion of the rural population who live within 2 km of an all-season road) is part of the vulnerability dimension it does not follow the logic “the higher the value the worse the circumstances”. A high value in this case is good and therefore we have to assign direction = -1 to this variable. In principle, the two dimensions vulnerability and exposure follow the predefined logic and coping capacity does not. The lower the values of coping capacity, the worse the circumstances. Thus, the default value for this dimension would be -1, although this does not apply to all indicators.
trans-unit.target: __Example 1:__ If the indicator “Rural Accessibility Index” (proportion of the rural population who live within 2 km of an all-season road) is part of the vulnerability dimension it does not follow the logic “the higher the value the worse the circumstances”. A high value in this case is good and therefore we have to assign direction = -1 to this variable. In principle, the two dimensions vulnerability and exposure follow the predefined logic and coping capacity does not. The lower the values of coping capacity, the worse the circumstances. Thus, the default value for this dimension would be -1, although this does not apply to all indicators.

trans-unit.source: __Example 2:__ For coping capacity we could have the following indicators: “number of schools”, “number of health sites”, ”travel time to nearest health facility”. The first two indicators “number of schools” and “number of health sites” follow the logic “the lower the value the worse the circumstances” thus they must be assigned direction = -1. The indicator ”travel time to nearest health facility” follows another logic (“the higher the value the worse the circumstances”) and thus must be assigned direction = 1.
trans-unit.target: __Example 2:__ For coping capacity we could have the following indicators: “number of schools”, “number of health sites”, ”travel time to nearest health facility”. The first two indicators “number of schools” and “number of health sites” follow the logic “the lower the value the worse the circumstances” thus they must be assigned direction = -1. The indicator ”travel time to nearest health facility” follows another logic (“the higher the value the worse the circumstances”) and thus must be assigned direction = 1.

trans-unit.source: It is recommended to properly check the logic of each indicator. Often the indicators of a certain dimension follow the same logic but there are always exceptions. After the directions have been applied to the data, we can speak of “lack of coping capacity” instead of “coping capacity” since we force the respective indicators in another direction following the predefined overarching logic (the higher the value = the worse the circumstances).
trans-unit.target: It is recommended to properly check the logic of each indicator. Often the indicators of a certain dimension follow the same logic but there are always exceptions. After the directions have been applied to the data, we can speak of “lack of coping capacity” instead of “coping capacity” since we force the respective indicators in another direction following the predefined overarching logic (the higher the value = the worse the circumstances).

trans-unit.source: The normalized and substituted indicators are summed up into three scores (Exposure, Vulnerability, Lack of Coping Capacity) by considering the user defined weights and directions. The weighted score for a specific variable is calculated using the following formula, where value is the value of the variable in the dataset and weight is the weight assigned to the variable.
trans-unit.target: The normalized and substituted indicators are summed up into three scores (Exposure, Vulnerability, Lack of Coping Capacity) by considering the user defined weights and directions. The weighted score for a specific variable is calculated using the following formula, where value is the value of the variable in the dataset and weight is the weight assigned to the variable.

trans-unit.source: If the __direction is 1__ (indicating a positive weight), the formula is straightforward:
trans-unit.target: If the __direction is 1__ (indicating a positive weight), the formula is straightforward:

trans-unit.source: If the __direction is -1__ (indicating a negative weight), the formula adjusts the value by subtracting it from 1 before applying the weight:
trans-unit.target: If the __direction is -1__ (indicating a negative weight), the formula adjusts the value by subtracting it from 1 before applying the weight:

trans-unit.source: The second formula inverts the value $(1 - value)$ before applying the weight, resulting in a different calculation for variables with negative weights.
trans-unit.target: The second formula inverts the value $(1 - value)$ before applying the weight, resulting in a different calculation for variables with negative weights.

trans-unit.source: After considering weights and directions, the weighted variables are summed up to scores for each dimension (Exposure, Vulnerability, Lack of Coping Capacity):
trans-unit.target: After considering weights and directions, the weighted variables are summed up to scores for each dimension (Exposure, Vulnerability, Lack of Coping Capacity):

trans-unit.source: The risk is calculated by the geometric mean of the dimensions Exposure and Susceptibility, while Susceptibility is defined by the geometric mean of Vulnerability and the Lack of Coping Capacity. The geometric mean is chosen since it offers the advantage of rewarding balanced developments and equal reduction of deficits at all levels of the model:
trans-unit.target: The risk is calculated by the geometric mean of the dimensions Exposure and Susceptibility, while Susceptibility is defined by the geometric mean of Vulnerability and the Lack of Coping Capacity. The geometric mean is chosen since it offers the advantage of rewarding balanced developments and equal reduction of deficits at all levels of the model:

trans-unit.source: $ susceptibility = \sqrt vulnerability \times lack\ of\ coping\ capacity $
trans-unit.target: $ susceptibility = \sqrt vulnerability \times lack\ of\ coping\ capacity $

trans-unit.source: If no coping capacity file has been provided, the risk is calculated as follows:
trans-unit.target: If no coping capacity file has been provided, the risk is calculated as follows:

trans-unit.source: Please remember that it is highly recommended to take the coping capacity into account.
trans-unit.target: Please remember that it is highly recommended to take the coping capacity into account.

trans-unit.source: A notable limitation of the system is that the self created “csv”-files must conform exactly to the specified format outlined in the provided examples. It is also important to know that no placeholders or default values can be assigned in the "csv"-files in the plugin application. Each cell must be filled with relevant data before the plugin is used. In general, many input files are required to use the plugin, which makes it highly prone to errors.
trans-unit.target: A notable limitation of the system is that the self created “csv”-files must conform exactly to the specified format outlined in the provided examples. It is also important to know that no placeholders or default values can be assigned in the "csv"-files in the plugin application. Each cell must be filled with relevant data before the plugin is used. In general, many input files are required to use the plugin, which makes it highly prone to errors.
